figures.caption: Fig. 4. Method-library hit rate versus repeat index in the self-execution repeated-task setting. The proposed method stores learned solutions locally after the first execution, leading to direct reuse from the second execution onward.

Fig. 5. Overall comparison of average total execution time in the observationdriven setting. The proposed observation-driven method is faster than Observation-Only while maintaining the same success rate.

Fig. 3. Average LLM calls versus repeat index in the self-execution repeatedtask setting. The proposed method requires LLM interaction only in the first execution, whereas Always-LLM depends on the LLM in every execution.

Fig. 1. Overall comparison of the three methods in the self-execution repeated-task setting, including average total execution time, average LLM calls, average LLM time ratio, and success rate. The proposed method achieves lower time cost and substantially lower LLM dependence than Always-LLM while maintaining the highest success rate.

Fig. 2. Average total execution time versus repeat index in the self-execution repeated-task setting. The proposed method is initially slower due to learning overhead, but becomes faster than Always-LLM from the second execution onward.

Fig. 8. Method-library hit rate versus repeat index in the observationdriven setting. The proposed method successfully converts observed successful behavior into reusable local methods from the second round onward.

Fig. 6. Average total execution time versus repeat index in the observationdriven setting. After the initial observation-and-learning round, the proposed method remains consistently faster than Observation-Only in later executions.

Fig. 7. Average LLM calls versus repeat index in the observation-driven setting. The proposed method requires LLM interaction only in the first round, whereas Observation-Only continues to depend on the LLM in later rounds.
